content: Liquidity risk has two forms: market liquidity risk (inability to trade without moving prices) and funding liquidity risk (inability to meet cash obligations). Market liquidity is measured by bid-ask spreads, market depth, and resilience. During stress, liquidity can evaporate rapidly—the 2008 crisis saw even Treasury markets experience dysfunction. Funding liquidity became critical when Lehman couldn't roll over short-term borrowing. Basel III introduced Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) requiring banks to hold enough high-quality liquid assets to survive 30 days of stress, and Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR) addressing longer-term mismatches. The March 2020 dash for cash showed liquidity risks persist despite regulations.
